repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Laguna Park was later re-named  Ruben Salazar Park after the L.A. Times journalist Ruben Salazar who was killed at the August 29, 1970 Chicano Moratorium.  The Moratorium took place at Laguna Park and extended into the community of East Los Angeles. Rosalio, Maria Rosalia, and Ricardo all attended the Moratorium.

Left to right: Neighbor (name is unknown), Rosalio Munoz (3 1/2 years old), Maria Rosalia Munoz, and Ricardo Munoz (5 years old). The photograph was taken by their father, Rosalio Munoz on Ricardo's 5th birthday at Laguna Park, in East Los Angeles, CA.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Left to right: Arturo C. Adame and Jose Luis Guaderrama (cousin-same age). Arturo's mother possibly took the photograph. It was taken in the front yard of the family's home on Carlson Lane in Redondo Beach, CA. This picture shows North Redondo Beach still largely undeveloped.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Left to Right:Annie Garica, Ginger Holguin, and Champ Fraga. The photograph was taken by Richard P. Holguin - Ginger Holguin's father, at her baptism. Both Annie and Champ are Ginger's godparents.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

title_tesim: Christmas at Asbury United Methodist Church
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Rosalio Munoz (4 years old) is the small boy wearing shorts in the front of the photograpsh, Mercedes Gutierrez (cousin) is directly behind him, Ricardo Munoz (brother) is the young boy puckering his cheeks, Felix Gutierrez (cousing) is the boy wearing the cowboy sweater in the front of the picture, and at the far right in the front of the photograph is Maria Rosalia Munoz (sister) with her eyes closed. The photograph was taken at a Christmas party for children attending Sunday school at Asbury United Methodist Church. This church was one of the early Methodist churches to integrate in 1950s Los Angeles.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Left to Right: Laura Murillo, Abel Murillo, Sonia Murillo, and Cesar Murillo on Christmas Morning. Laura Murrilo notes the unusual birthday look to Christmas morning, " My Father Juan Murillo was a baker and although he could not afford to buy the family gifts, he made sure to bake a cake for the family." Juan Murillo was the one who photographed this moment with his children showcasing their Christmas Cake.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

title_tesim: Yolanda recieves a master's degree in Education
location_tesim: California State University Los Angeles in Los Angeles, CA
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Left to Right: Juan Hernandez, Carmen Hernandez (Yolanda's Mom), Yolanda Roura, Cuauhtli Roura, Quiche Roura, Elias Hernandez, and Andrew Hernandez.This photograph was taken by Miguel Roura California State University Los Angeles during Yolanda's graduation. She recieved a master's degree in Education.This was the same year that the Olympics came to Los Angeles. Yolanda was the first in her family to receive not only a college degree, but a master's degree in Education.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

title_tesim: First Chicano UCLA student body president
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: The copyright holder for this photograph is the photographer - George Rodriguez.

This photograph is of Rosalio Munoz. It was taken in Kerkhoff Hall at the undergraduate press office at UCLA. This photograph was taken by George Rodriguez for an L.A. Times article on Chicano student activism. It documents Rosalio's election as the first Chicano UCLA student body president.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

title_tesim: Francisco Guerra de Carpio at the Comisaria de Policia
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: From left to right:Francisco Guerra de Carpio with two men who were possibly law enforcement officials or military servicemen in Mexico.The photograph was taken in front of the Comisaria de Policia (Police Headquarters) in Naco, Sonora, Mexico. Mr. Guerra was an aduanero (border patrol official) for the Mexican government at the Naco Sonora border entry point. This photograph is believed to have been taken in close proximity to his workplace.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

title_tesim: Arturo Guerra posing in front of someone else's car
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Arturo Guerra standing in front of a car in Boyle Heights, CA.Mr. Guerra loved cars. However, he never actually learned how to drive. This photograph was taken in front of a family member's car.Also, Mr. Guerra's clothes are similar to that popularly referred to as a zoot suit. Interestingly, he always said that he thought zuit suits "looked sloppy."
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

title_tesim: Family portrait taken for el dia de las madres
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Left to Right:Salome Monreal Valadez (daughter), Petra Valadez de Monreal (mother), and Maria Monreal Valadez (daughter). This family portrait was taken on el dia de las madres (mother's day) in a photography studio located in Allende, Coahuila, Mexico.
member_of_collections_ssim: Los Angeles Latino Families Photo Project

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Left to Right: Joaquin Morales (son), Manuel Morales (son),  Trinidad Morales (mother), and Andrea Corral (daughter, 7 years old). This photograph was taken in a studio for Andrea's first communion. At the time, the family was living in East Los Angeles in Maravilla on Kern Avenue.This image is a reproduction of the original photograph.

title_tesim: Sida peut se transmettre de 3 manières, et pas autrement [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: AIDS can be transmitted in three ways, and no others
publisher_tesim: Comité français d'éducation pour la santé
caption_tesim: Sida. Aujourd'hui on peut faire beaucoup. Mais rien sans vous
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Pour en parler, pour savoir où vous près de chez vous: SIDA Info Service: 0 800 840 800. Appel anonyme, confidentiel et gratuit.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster title is in black letters on teal background. Remainder of text is black, white or teal on a rose beige background. In the center is a panel of three small panes, each with a drawing and text to ways in which AIDS is transmitted: having sex without a condom when one person has the AIDS virus; from an infected mother to her baby during pregnancy, at delivery or while nursing; when drug users share needles.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR005.
Date on back of poster, 1999, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

alternative_title_tesim: Sida. Aujourd'hui, on peut faire beaucoup. Mais rien sans vous
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: AIDES-Provence et HB Productions present ... Dimanche matin à Marseille, 3 courts métrages de Mario Fanfani. Avec le soutien financier du Ministère de l’emploi et de la solidarité--Secrétariat d’état à la santé; du Comité français d’éducation pour la santé; de la DDASS des Bouches-du-Rhône; du Conseil Regional Provence-Alpes-Côte d’Azur; du Conseil Général des Bouches-du-Rhône, de la ville de Marseille; de l’Assistance Publique-Hôpitaux de Marseille.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Photo showing a wooden floor with a bag. Spilling from the bag are artifacts: plaid shorts, a billfold, pills, a subscription for AZT, and three photographs. The photo evokes scenes from the motion picture Dimanche matin à Marseille, which consists of three film shorts named after the main characters in each: Renaud, Catherine, Béranger. The film shorts cover episodes from the lives of three people whose lives have been touched by AIDS.
Credits: Mario Fanfani (director); Jean-Pierre Bacri, Christiane Cohendy, Charles Berling, Fatiha Cheriguene, Sara Louis, Jean-Benoît Terral.
Part of: "Sida. Aujourd'hui, on peut faire beaucoup. Mais rien sans vous" campaign [AIDS. Today, we can do a lot. But nothing without you]
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR025.
Date on back of poster, 1999, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

title_tesim: Procedure exceptionnelle, en l’absence de seringues neuves uniquement [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Exceptional procedure, only in the absence of new syringes
Sida. Aujourd'hui, on peut faire beaucoup. Mais rien sans vous
publisher_tesim: Comité français d'éducation pour la santé
condition_note_ssi: Additonal poster text: A défaut de seringues neuves uniquement. Désinfection des seringues usagées à l’eau de Javel. Réduit les risques de contamination par le virus du sida et des hépatites mais ne les élimine pas totalement. Lavez abondamment la seringue a l’eau courante des que possible apres l’injection, puis desinfectez...
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Depicts a syringe container, with instructions on how to disinfect them for re-use. Poster explains that these procedures should only be followed in the absence of clean, new syringes.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR024.
Part of: "Sida. Aujourd'hui, on peut faire beaucoup. Mais rien sans vous" campaign. [AIDS, today we can do a lot. But nothing without you]

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Quand on change de partenaire, le préservatif est, bien utilisé, la meilleure protection contre le virus du sida (VIH) et les maladies sexuellement transmissibles (MST).
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Photograph of a woman's top and a man's trousers thrown over the top of a purple chair in the foreground, with a window in the background. A lavender border surrounds the photo on all sides except the top. At the bottom right of the poster, below the text, is shown an unused condom.
Date on back of poster, 1997, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR007.

title_tesim: Ce qui suit va vous intéresser. Ça ne parle que de sexe [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: The following will interest you. It's about nothing but sex
caption_tesim: Protégez-vous du sida
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR004.

An artistic black and white photograph depicts the buttocks and back of a reclining nude figure (sex indistinguishable) and highlights the sensuous curves of buttocks and back. The figure is reclining with legs toward the left and upper body toward the right of the photograph. In the background are three light rectangles, which are probably windows.  Above the photo, the first two lines of text appear in large black lettering on a lavender background; following are four more lines of text in grey letters on white. Below the photo are 5 more lines of grey-on-white text, followed by the campaign slogan, "Protégez-vous du sida." The logo of the sponsoring ministry appears in the lower right corner.  The message of the text promotes safe sex by poetically talking about mutual exploration of the curves of the body, the pleasures of the eyes and of kissing, and so on.  It concludes by giving a phone number "if you want to talk about it," meaning other ways of practicing safe sex.

Part of: "Sida. Aujourd'hui, on peut faire beaucoup. Mais rien sans vous" campaign [AIDS. Today, a lot can be done--but nothing without you]

description_tesim: Part of the public AIDS campaign in Germany: Gib AIDS keine Chance [Don't give AIDS a chance].

The poster is part of a published brochure entitled "AIDS ist auch Männersache." Divided by color into quadrants, the poster features four pictures: condoms (on an orange background), a man's hand holding a small barbell (on a blue background), an open lipstick container (on a pink background), and four Teddy bears (on a green background). In the lower right corner, a small AIDS ribbon appears next to the logo for the German anti-AIDS campaign slogan.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW138.

title_tesim: Warum verlangt Leda das von mir?

repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW139.

Poster is a comical cartoon drawing of a swan, with a condom on its beak, lamenting "Why does Leda expect me to do this?" The obvious reference is to the classical Greek myth, "Leda and the Swan," in which Zeus transforms himself into a Swan in order to lay with Leda (and impregnate her). The condom symbolizes protection against pregnancy, and could also be considered a symbol of protection against AIDS and sexually transmitted diseases.
Part of the public AIDS campaigns in Germany: Gib AIDS keine Chance [Don't give AIDS a chance]
Date on back of poster, 1999, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

description_tesim: Poster has a short story for the words,Worries,  Hope, Memories, Support, Gossip. For worries, Ina is an infected single mother who worries about the care of her daughter when she dies. For hope, Margot and Peter have taken on four-year old Tobias whose parents died of AIDS, they hope there will be a cure soon. For memories, Marianne lost both her sons to AIDS, she is having a difficult time adjusting, even though they died some time ago. For support, Jorge was accompanied by his friend Marcus when Jorge found out he had AIDS; Marcus is supportive, non-judgmental and not HIV-infected. For gossip, Kai lives in a small town, the last time his brother, Manfred, visited his family, they were shocked at his sickly appearance. His parents worry about what the neighbors are thinking. Poster sums up their stories, bringing up issues and questions. AIDS is a big challenge to those infected and their friends and families. They're dealing with the situation. How to cope with the terminally ill. A loved one will die, they wonder why it happened to them and how they can help.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW137b.

title_tesim: Why alcohol, crack and other drugs can put you at risk for the AIDS virus. [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Why alcohol, crack and other drugs can put you at risk for the AIDS virus. They cloud your thinking. You already know that shooting up drugs and sharing needles put you at risk for AIDS. But actually, other drugs such as crack, alcohol and marijuana can put you at risk because they cloud your thinking. And when your thinking is clouded, you take foolish chances. When you're high, you may have sex. That's risky stuff. So think clearly. Don't risk AIDS. If you'd like more information on the relationship between AIDS and sexually transmitted diseases, call the National AIDS hotline. 1-800-342-AIDS. The hotline for the hearing impaired is 1-800-AIDS-TTY.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item US005.
The poster is a blurry image of two people.  It implies that drug and alcohol use can impaire the mind, leading to behavior that puts one at risk of HIV infection.
Part of "America responds to AIDS" campaign.

title_tesim: Why there's a connection between sexually transmitted diseases and AIDS [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Poster text: Why there's a connection between sexually transmitted diseases and AIDS. Simple. The same type of sexual behavior that can infect you with gonorrhea, gential herpes, syphilis, and other sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) can also infect you with something else... the AIDS virus. You get STDs by having sex with someone who is infected. That's bad enough. But if you continue the same unprotected sexual behavior, you could get something you can't cure. AIDS. If you'd like more information on the relationship between AIDS and sexually transmitted diseases, call the National AIDS hotline. 1-800-342-AIDS. The hotline for the hearing impaired is 1-800-AIDS-TTY.

This poster is targeted at black prostitutes and sex workers, many of whom emigrated to Italy's major cities from North Africa. The language of the text is in Italian, English and French so that it can be understood by women who came from other countries.

Most of the poster is a striking graphic of a woman's face, with white areas suggesting her forehead, nose and cheeks, and with blue-green eyeshadow and red lips, all against a dark brown background.  The cheeks, forehead, nose, eyes and lips are composed of very small graphics of female sex workers in various suggestive poses, but they cannot be seen distinctly until the poster is viewed close-up. The brown background suggests that the woman is black.

The poster depicts a grainy black and white photograph of two men, one lying face down and the other lying face down on top of him; only the heads and shoulders can be seen. Both men have their eyes closed, and their positions suggest that they are having anal intercourse. Each man is making an assumption about the other and whether he is HIV-positive or negative. Poster text suggests the importance of being informed about HIV to prevent contracting it.
Based on the San Francisco "Assumptions" campaign, the slogan reads, "Comment fais-tu pour savoir?" English version is: "How do you know what you know?" www.penses-y.ca

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: HIV is the virus that causes AIDS. It attacks the human immune system, leaving an infected person vulnerable to a number of serious illnesses and cancers. The virus lives in the blood, vaginal fluid and semen of infected people. But the virus does not survive for long in the open air and can only be passed on by certain kinds of direct contact.HIV is contracted through vaginal or anal sex without a condom, injection or injury with used needles, mother to unborn or newborn baby. HIV is not contracted through working with people with HIV or AIDS, coughs and sneezes, holding hands, insect bites, food or knives and forks, toilets or sinks, protected sex with proper use of a condom.

Poster depicts three sides of a die, with names of men ("LUCAS", "SACHA") on two of the sides, and "SIDA" [AIDS] on the third side. According to the publisher's website, the die is meant to suggest that failure to use protection during sex is to gamble with one's life. The three sides of the die are intended to raise the question "With whom did you spend the night?" And to suggest that one of the answers could be: "AIDS".

Etre safe, ce n’est pas baiser sans capote parce qu’on pense que son partenaire est lui aussi séropositif.Etre safe, c'est tenir à la vie, éviter les contaminations et les surcontaminations.Etre safe, c'est préférer ses maris et ses amants vivants. repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Translated poster text: To be safe, do not select partners based on their HIV test.To be safe, do not fuck without condoms because you think your partner is also HIV negative. To be safe, do not fuck without condoms because you think your partner is also HIV positive. To be safe is to hold on to life, avoiding infections and reinfections. To be safe is to prefer that husbands and lovers live. To be safe is to use condoms every time. To choose your partners based only on their HIV status is discrimination.
Poster presents some advice for safety in relationships and awareness of HIV. "NoKpote" (or "no capote") also seems to refer to bareback sex, or sex without condoms.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR267.

Two men are having sex while wearing silver space suits on the surface of an alien planet.Their gloves are discarded on the ground next to them. They are at the edge of a crater, and their space ship is visible on the other side of the crater. Another planet looms large in the sky.

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Etwas gesunder Weitblick fördert den Spaß am Sex. Denn wer immer und überall Kondome benutzt, ist immer und überall vor sexuell übertragbaren Krankheiten geschützt. So bleiben Ihnen unnötige Sorgen erspart - und der Genuss auf lange Sicht erhalten!
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Photo depicts a carrot with its tip encased in a condom.

Translated additional poster text: Somewhat healthy farsightedness promotes fun sex. After all, those who always and everywhere use condoms are always protected against sexually transmitted diseases. Thus you are spared unnecessary worry -- and receive the benefit in the long term!
Part of two public AIDS campaigns in Germany: Gib AIDS keine Chance [Don't give AIDS a chance], and Mach's mit [Join in].
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW265.

Image of a hypodermic syringe with it's cap off and set beside it.  Poster warns about the important of using new needles and syringes and how using used syringes can lead to diseases such as HIV and AIDS.  Poster also lists a number one can buy need needles and syringes at a pharmacy without a prescription.
